summary: Gruber reviews the 2022 iPad Air, focusing on how it compares to the 11-inch iPad Pro now that both share the M1 chip. He walks through the spec differences—Face ID vs Touch ID, ProMotion, speakers, storage tiers—and argues the meaningful price gap is only $150 at the comparable 256GB tier. He explains why the two models seem redundant right now but attributes this to Apple's staggered refresh cycles, predicting the gap will widen again when the Pro gets updated. He praises the Air as a bargain, especially at 64GB, but criticizes the landscape front-camera placement across all iPads.
key_points: At the only shared storage tier (256GB), the iPad Air and iPad Pro are separated by just $150, making the comparison tighter than the $600 vs $800 base prices suggest
The M1 chip upgrade makes the iPad Air and 11-inch iPad Pro feel redundant, but Apple's staggered 18-month refresh cycles naturally create and close gaps between models
Face ID vs Touch ID is the most meaningful daily-use difference, especially when the iPad is in a Magic Keyboard
The front-facing camera placement remains a significant design problem for landscape video calls across all iPad models
money_quotes: Touch ID on the iPad Air makes it feel easy to unlock. Face ID on the iPad Pro makes it seems like it wasn't even locked in the first place.
Right now, with the brand-new iPad Air upgraded to the same M1 chip that's in the iPad Pro, it looks like there's little reason for the 11-inch iPad Pro to exist.
I have never understood why Apple doesn't want to call that '13 inches'. Go look at a ruler: a tenth of an inch (~2.5mm) is negligible.
Those Qualcomm modems aren't free and Apple isn't eating the cost.
If you look at the camera you're not looking at the screen, and if you look at the screen the camera makes it look like you're not looking at the screen.
I don't know what the solution to this problem is, but Apple hasn't found it yet.
Someday this may change, but to date, the only devices that support ProMotion literally have 'Pro' in their names: iPad Pro, iPhone Pro, and MacBook Pro.
key_insight: The 2022 iPad Air's M1 upgrade narrows the gap with the 11-inch iPad Pro to a mere $150 at comparable storage, making it the better value for most users until Apple's staggered refresh cycle widens the gap again.
